Less luNilze, hut even more ferocious,
the leopard has a worse character than
the tiger or lion Living mainly in
trees and very nocturnal, this fierce
and dangerous beast is less often seen
than far rarer animals. It Is Widely
spread over tho world from tho Cape
of Good IIopc to tho Atlas mountains
and from southern China to the Black
sea, whero It is sometimes met with In
the Caucasus.
Any one who has frequented the coo
for any time must have noticed the
difference In 6lzo and color between
leopards from different parts of the
world. On eouio the ground color Is
almost white, In others a clear nut
brown. Others are Jet black.
Wherever they live leopards are cat
tle thieves, sheep thieves, dog thieves
and human Iteing thieves. Though not
formidable In appenrauce, they are Im
mensely strong, and it Is not unusual
for them to turn man eater. Doth In
India and In Africa they have been
known to set up In this line as delib
erately as any tiger. They have four
or five young at a birth. The cubs can
be kept tame for some time and are)
amusing pets, but it Is extremely dan
gerous to have them about
In Hongkong an Englishman had a
tame leopard. It was brought Into tb
dining room by a coolie to be exhibit
ed to the owner's guests. Excited by
the smell of food, the leopard refused
to go out when one of the women, who
did not like his looks, asked that it be
removed. Tho coolie took holl of Ifs
collar and began, to haul It out It
seized him by the ucck. bit it through
and In a minute the coolie was dying,
covered wlih blood, on the dining
room floor.
Tho Chlnose leopard ranges as for
north as the Siberlun tiger and, like
the latter. Fcems to grow larger the
farther north It Is found. The cor
of these northern leopards Is very pal,
the spots ore large iid the fur Is very
long.
The natives of all countries ar
Imous In declaring that the leopard i.
more dangerous than the Hon or tiger.
Thoy have no fonr of the Hon. provided
they are not hunting for It. for It wi'J
not attack unless provoked, but a
leopard is never to be trusted.
In Africa a number of natives were
Bring the reeds along u Btreani. One of
them, & boy. being thirsty and hot.
stooped down to drink. lie was Imme
diately seized by a leopanl. The boy's
brother, with un admirable aim, hurled
bis spear at the leopard while the boy
was in his jawt. The point separated
the vertebrae of the neck, and the
leopard fell stone dead. Hut the boy
could not recover. The leopard's fangs
had torn open his chest and injured the
lungs. The latter were exposed to
view through the cavity of the ribs
lie died during the night
Leopard are essentially tree living
and nocturnal animals. Sleeping in
trees or caves by day, they are seldom
disturbed. They do an incredible
amount of mischief among cattle,
calves, sheep and dogs, being especial
ly fond of killing and eating the latter-
They seize their prey by the throat
and cling with their claws until they
succeed In breaking the spine or In
strangling the victim. They have a
habit of feeding on putrid flesh. This
makes wounds inflicted by their teeth
or claws liable to bbod poisoning.
Nothing in the way at prey comes
amiss to them, from a cow InHbe pas
ture to a fowl up at roost
In the great mountain ranges of cen
tral Asia the beautiful snow leopard Is
found. It Is a large creature, with
thick, woolly coat aud a long tall like
a furboa. The color is white, clouded
with beautiful gray, like that of an
Angora cat The edges of the cloud
logs and spots are marked with black
or darker gray. The eyes are very
large, bluish gray or smoke colored.
It lives on the wild sheep, ibex and
other mountain animals. In captivity
It Is far the tamest andentlest of the
large carnlvora. not excepting the pu
ma. Unlike the latter, it is a sleepy,
quiet animal, like a, domestic.
The West African leopard skin is
more handsome than the Asiatic, the
spots being very distinct and clear. lie
and she hey usually go in couples
are fond of hunting cantonments and
around native towns, where they pick

Burdjns That innocent English Chil

In Mngiand, as" it. other countries,
thousands of poop!;; p tlirfitiuh life
cherishiug a gritilp' afalnst their par

gruous names. It was nyst natural
that a demure and pretty girl in a
north suburb should f:el resentful
when she had to answer to the name
of ltusybody, given in honor of the
winner of a race fifteen years lefore.
Among the names registered at Som
erset House are Air-nd (Jraces and
Nun Nicer, which were innocently
borne by two little girls who found
them most embarrassing in after years.
The appalling name of Wellington
Volsoley Koberts was borne by a
young man who. In disposition and ap
pearance, was anything but militant.
and as little likely to win fame on
the battlefield as his predecessors Ar
thur Wellesley Wellington Waterloo
fox and Napoleon the Great Kagar.
However, even these names, Inap
propriate as" they may be, are to be
preferred to Roger the Ass, Anna (sic)
poniinl Da vies and Euadicea Basher.
To parents of large families the ad
vent ofanother child is not nlways
welcome, but it Is scarcely kind to
make the unexpected child bear a tok
en of disapproval. It must be rather
terrible to go through life, for exam
ple; ns Not Wanted James, What An
other, Onty Fancy William Brown, or
even as Last of 'Km Harper, or Still
Another Hewitt. And yet these are
all names which the foolish caprice
of British parents hub imposed on In
nocent children. Chicago Kecord-IIer-ald.

Two French gowns displayed recent

The Premet model Is one of those

REALLY DESERVING OF PITY
Those Who Allow Themselves to Get
Into the Habit of Self-Excuse
Are Unfortunate.
i The tendency to self-excuse Is typi
cal of the conceited. They simply will
not see themselves for what they are
persons who have a great deal to
learn. And because they will not ad
mit even to themselves that they need
self-Improvement they remain perpet
ually In need of it
- Are not persons thus "conditlone-i
more deserving of pity than of eon
tempt? Even their monumental self
satisfaction should not make people
laugh at them. Poor souls! Life to
them Is a game of blind man's buff.
In which they are forever "It." forever
groping wildly, but never grasping.
No truer words have been penned
than Kuskin's. "Conceit may puff a
man up. but not prop him up." And
how sadly hard It Is to convince the
conceited that they ore conceited, and
thus save them frtm the painful penal
ties of conceit. II. Addington Uruce
In Chicago Daily News.

U. S. EMPLOYES HIT

Welch-Smoot Measure Criti
cized and Speakers Heckled j
at Public Meeting.
DtMTsardms a warning by Luther C. :
Steward, president of the National lod
erauon ot Federal Employes, that at
tempts to amend the Smoot-Welrb sal
ary increase bill may defeat It. several j
hundred Government employes, mooting
at Central High School last night, enti
eised the bill, heckled speakers, includ- j
tag Senator Blame of Wisconsin, and
finally passed a resolution asking sharp ,
amendment m the measure. Senator j
Blaine indicated he would work for j
changes m the bill to liberalize it
Mrs Margaret Hopkins Worrell, loader :
In the recent march on the Capitol,
who engineered the meeting ar.d hurled
a dofi at th* federation and Steward,
planned to take the resolution today
to the Senate committee on th** civil
service, which is holding hearings on
the bill
Resigns From Federation.
She said she had sent resignation
to the federation and would support
thr United Stales Employes' Associa- j
tton. in behalt of which Charles A
Doven. an organizer from San Fran
cisco. addressed the meeting. Applies- >
tion blanks were distributed for this
organization, started five years ago, and j
It was said efforts would be made to !
revive and strengthen it to work espe- j
cially for the • underpaid' - m the Gov- *
eminent service ... , „„ !
The resolution adopted last night, on
motion of Mrs Worrell criticized both
Steward and the Welch bill as doing ;
too much for the highly-paid and too ;
little for lower-paid employes, and pro- •
posed two amendments to the salary j
measure. I
Amendments Suggested.
First it asked the committee to
Change the bill so that -employes under
the $4,000 limit, in salaries will receive;
• flat increase of S3OO as an emergency ;
measure.” . ... .... „ !
The resolution also asked the bill re- ,
tain the "relative position" provision,
authorize appropriations to take care
of the increases provided, and disre- ;
tarried the "average provision of the
classification act, so as to insure pro- :
motion. , . .
Resentment against what was claimed
to be discrimination in favor of higher j
paid employes, as against the lower j
paid, was in evidence throughout the
meeting.
Steward's Warning Quoted.
The warning bv steward against the
meeting, it was disclosed was distribut
ed to the presidents and secretaries
of all Washington locals of the feder
ation over the signature of Steward.
The letter referred to “misguided and
destructive efforts," which could only
ierve to "defeat the enactment of salary ;
Increase legislation at the present ses- j
glon of Congress." and declared it to
be “our duty to advise you to urge all j
members of the National Federation j
of Federal Employes to refrain from at- j
tending the meeting at Central High
School.” Steward warned that "pro- i
longed discussion, agitation, or at-!
tempts to amend, in the interest of j
Individuals or groups, will. It may j
•uthorativelv be stated, serve only to
delav. impede and perhaps defeat the j
MU.”
Senator Blaine's Comments.
Senator Blaine explained the pro
visions of the bill and declared there
seer- two principal objections.
The bIU. the Senator said, had been
understood by the service to be for the
benefit of aIL whereas he thought it will
•benefit only the new employes and
those least entitled to it." |
fa the second place, he thought the
operation of the average provision of
the classification act would react
through administrative procedure in
holding down the increases intended by 1 1
the legislation. As s matter of fact he
thought that tome employes probably
mould be "graded backwards instead of;
forwards " He thought the bill should j
contain a provision automatically pro- j I
moting persona so as to prevent the I
average provision from holding back
Increases Theoretically the bill pro- j
vide* for about $1*.000.000 increase, he j
•aid but a "responsible authority" had
estimated tor him that actually the in-1
creases to be received under reclassifi
cation might only reach $6,000,000. j
Resents Heckling.
He resented the suggestion of "threat j
0f disapproval either by three who run-;
trol Congress, or by a veto." When
heckled by a white-haired Government <
employe who wanted to know. "Is the
House or Senate of the United State#,
In the habit of taking instructions'
from any one for the appropriation of
tnoney?" The Senator responded
tartly, "I can't speak for the Senate
or House, but I’m not In the habit of
taking instructions from any one."
Representative La Guardia. of New
Tors. In addressing the meeting, de
clared he favored fixing a minimum
Government salary of *I,BOO, and said
It was "positively indecent for the *
Government to have an unmarried girl
working for leas than *1.500 a year”;
He characterized it as a courageous j
thing if the meeting would serve notice ;i

text:
THE PACIFIC COMMERCIAL ADVERTISER: HONOLULU, SEPTEMBER 21, 1897.
TEA WILL
GROW
So Says
an Experienced
tivator.
Cnl-
JAMES HUTCHINS OPIHIOH
Climate of Hilo Better
Than That of Ceylon.
Hlgh-Prlced Labor May be an Ob
stacleMr. Forest's
Success.
"I consider the climate of Hawaii
superior to that of Ceylon" said James
Ilutcbins last night, "and for tnat rea
son, coupled of course with the fact
that the soil and conditions are rlgftt
I believe a superior quality of tea can
be raised on the island of Hawaii than
can be produced in any part of India
Cevlon included, I say Ceylon included
because the tea of Ceylon, owing to a
difference in the climate, is of superior
flavor."
Mr. Hutchins was engaged in the
cultivation of tea in Cochar, Province
of Assam, on the Bengal coast. He is
familiar with every detail of the work
and after a careful investigation of the
conditions in the Hilo district, he ex
presses himself favorably of the pros
pect for tea cultivation on that part of
the island.
'I did not go to Kona, so I can give
no opinion of the conditions which ex
ist there. In the Hilo district the soil,
climate and rain fall are all that a tea
grower could wish for. I saw at the
ranch of Mr. Forest at Kaumanu a small
patch of tea only 15 months old that
Is really better than any tea plants in
India three or four months older. I
saw no reason why the coffee planters
on Hawaii should not add tea to their
products.
"Of course there's the difficulty of
labor to contend with; with tea as
with coffee and sugar, three of the
most important of the world's staples,
cheap labor is essential. Coolie labor,
I mean Indian coolie labor, is available
and should be employed in the work.
During my 18 months' residence on Ha
waii I had ample opportunity to study
the labor problem for I saw working
every day in the same field Portuguses,
young and old, Chinese and Japanese
The grown Portuguese are paid about
$20 per month, boys average $11 per
month and do about 15 per cent, work
less in a day than the men. Asiatics
get $15 or less per month and are as
far behind the Portuguse boys as the
boys are behind their elders. An Indian
coolie will get ?2 per month, will work
steadily and keep ahead of the Asiatics
every day of the year.
"In India we give the laborer a cer
tain task to perform in a day; if he
fails he dees not get his full wage, if
he does more than his task he gets
extra pay. Those who do not care to
work overtime in the field, repair to
their homes and make baskets and
nick-nacks which they dispose of to
the merchants in the nearest town.
There is no shirking work, because
they want all the money they can
earn. Their morals? Why, far above
the Asiatics in every respect, and the
chastity of the wife is respected if for
no other reason than that the husband
very promptly and effectually resents
an insult offered her. From my experi
ence in India and from what I have
seen here I am satisfied that better re
sults may be obtained by setting tasks
for the laborers and making the task
a long one, if necessary, than under
the present system. A luna or a man
ager could take as a sample the day's
work of half a dozen of the ablest men
on the plantation, and establishing that
as a task, the other laborers would
have to keep up to it, and if they failed
their pay should be decreased.
"Having been interested in tea cul
tivation, I would like to see it more
generally taken up on these Islands,
provided the labor problem can be
solved. The class of labor now on the
Islands is too high-priced for tea, and
is not, I take it, just what is needed as
employes on coffee plantations. Fam
ilies are required for the latter, and a
determined effort should be made to
secure them.
"You understand, of course, that the
little patches of tea on Hawaii, as far
as I have seen, are grown by novices.
They have much to learn regarding
the trimming and cultivation of the
plants. If left alone, they will grow
too tall, and without being of any bene
fit or advantage over the lower plant;
they outgrow their strength, so to
speak. And when the leaves are picked
one most important factor in the pro
duction of tea is the firing; that is an
art in itself, and must be learned. The
entire crop could be ruined by poor
firing."
Japanese Bronze.
A late dispatch says that scientists
have discovered the composition of
Japanese copper bronzes and alloys,
which was secretly guarded as a trade
secret by the wily Japs. The oldest
Japanese bronze, called "shadko," con
sists of 100 parts of copper alloyed
with from 1 to 10 parts of gold. The
blue-black color is produced by using
sulphite of copper, alum and verdigris.
The famous gray "gulshi-buishi" is
composed of copper to which from 30
to 50 per cent of silver has been added.
"Sinchu," the best Japanese brass,
contains 100 parts of copper and 50
parts of zinc; while "karakane," their
favorite bell metal, is the product of
100 parts copper, 15 parts zinc, 40 parts
tin and 5 parts iron. That beautiful
bronze called "mokume," which has an
indescribable metallic sheen, is a com
posite of composites, gold, silver,
"shadko," "guishi-buishi" and other in
gredients being used in its alloy.

In the summer the temperature of
cave near Decorah, Iowa, is far below
freezing, and from Its walls icicles de
pend like stalactites. In the winter
the temperature of the cave is like that
of summer.
